source.title: Ethylene Release and Fire at Kuraray America, Inc. EVAL Plant

actions_taken: Emergency pressure-relief valve closed after pressure dropped, extinguishing fire
Emergency responders transported injured workers to off-site medical facilities
Post-incident: EVAL Reactor 2 emergency pressure-relief system outlet piping redesigned to discharge vertically upward at 90 feet elevation
Post-incident: Emergency open valve automated to open on High-High-Pressure alarm
Post-incident: Plant-wide emergency alarm activated when reactor pressure exceeds high-pressure alarm threshold during startup
Post-incident: 50-foot exclusion zone established around each EVAL Reactor during startup
Post-incident: New safe operating limits established for EVAL Reactor 2
Post-incident: Operating procedure updated to clarify VOC permit limits disregarded during emergency
event.summary: At 10:28 a.m. on May 19, 2018, during startup of EVAL Reactor 2 following a maintenance turnaround at Kuraray America's EVAL plant in Pasadena, Texas, high-pressure conditions developed inside the reactor, activating the emergency pressure-relief system. The system discharged 2,347 pounds of flammable ethylene vapor in less than three minutes through horizontally aimed outlet piping toward an area where nonessential contract workers were performing maintenance activities including welding. An operating welding machine on a pickup truck likely ignited the ethylene vapor cloud, causing a fire that injured 23 workers. Contributing factors included: the unsafe horizontal discharge design of the emergency pressure-relief system; presence of nonessential workers during startup; disabled High-High-Pressure safety interlock; inadequate alarm management (approximately 160 alarms/hour); operators unaware of EVAL Reactor 2's lower design pressure (740 psi vs 1,150 psi for other reactors); nightly operating instructions conflicting with written procedures; operators limiting flare flow to avoid exceeding environmental VOC permit limits; and multiple failed or ineffective safeguards.
recommendations: 2018-03-I-TX-R1: Develop and implement an emergency pressure-relief system design standard to ensure discharge to a safe location, with periodic evaluation and modification requirements.
2018-03-I-TX-R2: Implement a site-wide system to evacuate nonessential personnel during upset conditions and exclude them during transient operating modes such as startup.

2018-03-I-TX-R3: Develop and implement a system requiring periodic evaluation of safeguard adequacy and effectiveness using safeguard protection analysis based on Cal/OSHA PSM for Petroleum Refineries or equivalent.

2018-03-I-TX-R4: Develop and implement a policy for effectively addressing recommendations from process safety management systems, consistent with OSHA guidance, including periodic training for managers.
2018-03-I-TX-R5: Review CCPS guidance on catastrophic incident warning signs and develop a program incorporating warning signs into the safety management system.
2018-03-I-TX-R6: Clarify the lower equipment design pressure of EVAL Reactor 2 within operator training systems, written procedures, and the control system interface.

2018-03-I-TX-R7: Develop a program to ensure nightly operating instructions do not conflict with written operating procedures; use MOC system for procedure changes; develop written procedure and training for methanol flush operation.

2018-03-I-TX-R8: Strengthen operator training program to include alarm setpoints and responses, emergency open valve guidance, safe operating limits, nonessential personnel exclusion criteria, and consequences of deviations.
2018-03-I-TX-R9: Complete alarm management efforts and implement a continual program to meet ISA 18.2 alarm rate performance targets.

2018-03-I-TX-R10: Improve safety management system by controlling chilled liquid circulation during startup, enhancing recognition of liquid ethylene accumulation, implementing abnormal operating conditions management, updating startup procedures for alarm response, using control system guidance for upset conditions, and removing restrictions on emergency open valve use.
2018-03-I-TX-R11: Modify safe operating limits program to prevent reliance on ASME code material safety factors.
2018-03-I-TX-R12: Acquire independent third-party services to perform comprehensive assessment of EVAL Plant process safety management systems.

hazards.name: High-pressure flammable ethylene inventory in EVAL Reactor 2
Emergency pressure-relief system discharging to unsafe location (horizontally aimed piping)

threats.name: Liquid ethylene accumulation in reactor due to premature chilled liquid circulation during startup
Heating of reactor contents (steam injection to jacket water) vaporising liquid ethylene and generating overpressure
Nightly operating instructions conflicting with written procedures, targeting higher pressure (595 psi vs 495 psi limit)
High-High-Pressure safety interlock disabled during troubleshooting of misaligned valve
Operators unaware of EVAL Reactor 2 lower design pressure (740 psi vs 1,150 psi)
Alarm flood preventing effective operator response (~160 alarms/hour)
Environmental VOC permit concern limiting flare venting by board operators

consequences.name: Ethylene vapor cloud formation at grade level near workers
Ignition of ethylene vapor cloud causing flash fire
23 workers injured (burns, fractures, sprains) including life-threatening burns to one worker
Release of 2,347 pounds of ethylene to atmosphere
Potential vapor cloud explosion risk to surrounding community

controls.name: Emergency Pressure-Relief System (rupture disc and spring-loaded relief valve)
controls.barrier_role: Prevent reactor rupture by relieving excess pressure
controls.lod_basis: Last line of defense against equipment rupture; activated when all other pressure controls failed

supporting_text: For scenarios in which these pressure control systems could not maintain control of the EVAL Reactor pressure, Kuraray equipped the EVAL Reactor with an emergency pressure-relief system intended to prevent the reactor from rupturing under high-pressure conditions.
An emergency pressure-relief system is a layer of protection that is often considered the last line of defense to prevent equipment rupture due to a high-pressure situation.

controls.name: High-High-Pressure Safety Interlock (automatic isolation of feeds and chilled liquid valve opening)
controls.barrier_role: Automatically isolate reactor feeds and open chilled liquid valve when pressure reaches 640 psi
controls.lod_basis: Automated safety interlock identified as PHA safeguard; second line of defense after pressure alarms

supporting_text: During the EVAL Reactor 2 startup, Kuraray operators disabled the reactor's abnormal condition safety interlock while troubleshooting a problem that stemmed from a misaligned valve.
Board Operator 1 turned the reactor High-High-Pressure safety interlock to the 'off' position. As the startup continued, this interlock remained off.

A second safeguard, the reactor's abnormal condition (High-High-Pressure) safety interlock, which used automatic controls to protect the reactor from high-pressure conditions, was not effective because it was disabled during the startup.

controls.name: High-Pressure and High-High-Pressure Alarms with Operator Response
controls.barrier_role: Alert board operators to high-pressure conditions for corrective action
controls.lod_basis: First PHA-identified safeguard: high-pressure alarms with operator opening emergency open valve

supporting_text: At 8:51 a.m., the pressure inside EVAL Reactor 2 reached 620 psi, activating the High-Pressure alarm. Ten minutes later, at 9:01 a.m., the reactor's High-High-Pressure alarm went off at 640 psi.

The first safeguard required the board operator to open the emergency open valve to lower the reactor pressure by sending vapor from the reactor to the flare. Although three operations personnel looked at the control system on the morning of the incident, the design of the alarm system, along with the startup environment, did not result in effective actions being taken.

The control system sent about 160 alarms per hour to the board operators on the morning of the incident. This flood of alarms contributed to the incident by preventing board operators from effectively reviewing the process information and controlling the high-pressure conditions inside EVAL Reactor 2.
Kuraray's operating procedures did not include alarm information or operator guidance on responding to process alarms.

controls.name: Pressure Control Valve to Flare (manual venting of reactor vapor)
controls.barrier_role: Reduce reactor pressure by directing vapor to flare system
controls.lod_basis: Primary pressure control mechanism available to board operators

supporting_text: To avoid exceeding an environmental permit emissions limit on non-combusted volatile organic compounds (VOCs) exiting the flare, Board Operator 1 limited how much he opened the pressure control valve.
Board Operator 2 opened the pressure control valve from 0 to 45 percent open to send more reactor vapor to the flare. The pressure inside the reactor was 716 psi.

Although Kuraray's board operators made adjustments that slowed the rate at which the pressure inside the reactor increased, the pressure inside EVAL Reactor 2 continued increasing and never dropped below the high-pressure alarm conditions.

controls.name: Emergency Open Valve to Flare
controls.barrier_role: Rapidly reduce reactor pressure by directing large volume of vapor to flare
controls.lod_basis: PHA-identified safeguard; last resort before emergency pressure-relief system activation

supporting_text: The operating procedure stated, 'Do not open [the emergency open valve] without supervisor approval.' In addition, the emergency open valve was activated using a physical switch on a panel board and was not part of the computer control system. Kuraray management installed a protective cap over the switch and locked the cap closed with a plastic hasp.
Neither board operator sought permission to open the emergency open valve before the emergency pressure-relief system was activated.
Had I known exactly what [pressure] that [emergency pressure-relief system] was going to relieve at, I could have opened up the [emergency open valve] and relieved it a lot faster.
Kuraray postponed upgrading and automating the emergency open valve to 2019.

controls.name: High-Pressure Switch (isolate reactor feeds)
controls.barrier_role: Automatically close valves to isolate reactor feeds on high pressure
controls.lod_basis: Third PHA-identified safeguard for controlling high-pressure conditions

supporting_text: The third safeguard, the high-pressure switch, also was not effective because the board operator had already closed the valves to stop the feeds from entering the reactor, and the continually increasing pressure inside EVAL Reactor 2 was not caused by ethylene flowing into the reactor. Rather, the inventory of liquid ethylene being warmed inside the reactor was generating ethylene vapor and driving the increasing pressure inside the reactor system.

controls.name: Safe Operating Limits Program
controls.barrier_role: Define pressure/temperature limits and predetermined corrective actions to prevent overpressure
controls.lod_basis: OSHA PSM-required process safety information element; intended to guide operator response before reaching design limits

supporting_text: Kuraray set the safe operating limit for EVAL Reactor 2 at 740 psi and 203°F—the reactor's mechanical design conditions.

Kuraray's safe operating limits did not consider the activation pressure of its emergency pressure-relief systems and the fact that these safety systems do not precisely activate at their design conditions.

Kuraray's operator training materials did not address safe operating limits, the consequence of deviating beyond the safe operating limits, or the predetermined steps that operators must take to return the process to a safe condition.
The consequence of deviation occurred before the safe operating limit was reached.

controls.name: Written Operating Procedures for Reactor Startup
controls.barrier_role: Provide clear instructions for safely conducting startup activities including pressure limits
controls.lod_basis: OSHA PSM-required administrative control; first line of defence for safe startup operations

supporting_text: Kuraray management supplied its operations team with nightly operating instructions that conflicted with the company's written operating procedures and resulted in unmanaged changes during the reactor startup.
The nightly operating instructions that Kuraray management provided its board operators targeted a reactor pressure that was 100 psi higher than what the company's operating procedures called for.
Kuraray's operating procedures did not include alarm information or operator guidance on responding to process alarms.
Although Kuraray performed a methanol flush of its EVAL Reactors after some maintenance activities, the company did not have a written procedure or train its operators to perform the methanol flush.

controls.name: Operator Training Program
controls.barrier_role: Communicate process knowledge, alarm responses, safe operating limits, and emergency actions to operators
controls.lod_basis: OSHA PSM-required training element; foundational administrative control for safe operations

supporting_text: Kuraray's operator training program did not cover alarm setpoints or actions that operators should take in response to specific process alarms, such as high-pressure conditions inside an EVAL Reactor.

I've never received that specific instruction. … I don't want to say it's a judgment call … but … at a certain point, if the [pressure control valve to the flare] isn't working, that's your last resort before the [emergency pressure-relief system activates].
Board Operator 2 was not aware of how close the reactor system's pressure was to the activation pressure design (740 psi) of the emergency pressure-relief system.

controls.name: Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) Program
controls.barrier_role: Identify, evaluate, and control process hazards including emergency pressure-relief system risks
controls.lod_basis: OSHA PSM and EPA RMP required periodic safety review; management system control

supporting_text: Kuraray's 2015 PHA team found that potential ethylene releases from some of the site's emergency pressure-relief systems (including EVAL Reactor 2) could result in a flash fire or a vapor cloud explosion with worker fatalities.

The PHA team recommended that Kuraray perform a study to evaluate these potential ethylene releases from emergency pressure-relief systems and their safety impact on personnel. Kuraray management did not accept the PHA team's recommendation.
Kuraray declined to implement the safety recommendation, and consistent with the company's safety policy, its management team did not document its evaluation or reasoning for doing so.
Kuraray's 2015 PHA team evaluated low-temperature conditions that might develop in EVAL Reactor 2 as an operability concern, but the team documented no safety concern or hazard from such conditions.

controls.name: Nonessential Personnel Exclusion Practice (unwritten)
controls.barrier_role: Reduce personnel exposure during hazardous startup and upset conditions
controls.lod_basis: Informal site practice to exclude nonessential workers during initial ethylene introduction; no formal policy for full startup duration

supporting_text: Although Kuraray had an unwritten safety practice to exclude nonessential personnel from a unit when reintroducing (pressuring or charging) ethylene into equipment after a turnaround, the company did not establish a formal exclusion zone to protect nonessential workers for the duration of the startup.
At the time of the incident, none of the contract workers near the EVAL Reactor 2 emergency pressure-relief system were essential to the startup.

Kuraray's process safety management systems did not consider the high-pressure conditions developing inside EVAL Reactor 2 as an upset condition that should halt the maintenance work and prompt an evacuation of nonessential workers.

controls.name: Emergency Pressure-Relief System Discharge Design (safe location)
controls.barrier_role: Ensure discharged flammable vapor is directed away from personnel to a safe location
controls.lod_basis: Fundamental engineering design requirement per API 521, ASME, and industry RAGAGEP; intended to prevent harm when relief system activates

supporting_text: The primary breakdown at Kuraray was that the outlet piping from the EVAL Reactor 2 emergency pressure-relief system did not discharge the flammable ethylene vapor to a safe location.

Among the many process safety management system failures that led to injuring the 23 workers at Kuraray, none was more significant than the design of the EVAL Reactor 2 emergency pressure-relief system's outlet piping.

The emergency pressure-relief system for EVAL Reactor 2 was designed to discharge reactor vapor to the ambient air (atmosphere) about 40 feet above the ground... this piping design detail directed flammable ethylene vapor through the horizontally aimed piping and toward an area where many workers were performing maintenance activities.

controls.name: Hot Work Permit and Ignition Source Control
controls.barrier_role: Prevent ignition of flammable vapors by controlling hot work activities near hazardous areas
controls.lod_basis: Standard hot work permit system; intended to eliminate ignition sources near flammable materials

supporting_text: The welding work they were performing likely supplied the ignition source that created the fire.
The Harris County Fire Marshal's Office identified the ignition source as 'located in or around the operating welder [welding machine] located in the back of the Dodge pickup'.
Workers were performing various tasks that included pump demolition, hydrotesting, welding, insulating, and scaffolding. None of these workers were essential to the startup.

controls.name: Alarm Management System
controls.barrier_role: Optimise alarm quality and frequency to enable effective operator response to abnormal conditions
controls.lod_basis: ISA 18.2 compliant alarm management; administrative and engineering control to support operator decision-making

supporting_text: At the time of the incident, Kuraray had not completed its process alarm management efforts to improve the quality and reduce the frequency of alarms.
The control system sent about 160 alarms per hour to the board operators on the morning of the incident.
In the 3 hours and 28 minutes leading up to the incident, Kuraray's process control system inundated board operators with 552 alarms.

Kuraray's team's analysis of existing alarm conditions found that during a nearly one-month period in November 2016, the control system activated more than 265,000 alarms with an average alarm rate of 395 alarms per hour.

controls.name: Safety Management System Self-Assessment Audits
controls.barrier_role: Periodically evaluate process safety management systems to identify and correct weaknesses
controls.lod_basis: OSHA PSM and EPA RMP required compliance audit every three years

supporting_text: Kuraray's self-assessment audits did not achieve the level of detail required to address the process safety management system failures that contributed to the incident.

During Kuraray's management review, only 37 of the 64 proposed recommendations were accepted and assigned corrective actions and due dates to track completion (58 percent). Kuraray management did not accept the other 27 proposed recommendations (42 percent).
Kuraray management did not document its reasons for not accepting 42 percent of the recommendations proposed by its audit team.

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
